text: The surgical team places ports, including an 11mm assistant port, in preparation for robot docking, as visible in the setup image.
The surgeon begins the operation by creating a roolim, ensuring it is positioned without tension in the supracholic compartment.
The surgeon divides the mesentery close to the bowel, separating the connective tissue from the intestinal structure.
The surgeon widens the gap to facilitate the placement of a linear cutting stapler, which is then used to divide the bowel and confirm the orientation of the root.
The surgeon uses scissors with electrocautery to create enterotomies on both sides in preparation for the jejuno-jejunostomy.
The surgeon uses a bowel grasper to widen the gap between the bowel segments, preparing the area for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon creates a corresponding enterotomy and uses a linear cutting stapler to form the jejuno-jejunostomy, visibly connecting the two sections of the jejunum.
The surgeon closes the enterotomy of the common limb using a running IV or V-lock stitch, followed by a seromuscular III or vicryl stitch, ensuring secure closure of the bowel opening.
The surgeon begins dissection in the supracolic compartment, focusing on separating tissue layers and structures within this area.
The video shows inflammation and adhesions near the gallbladder, likely resulting from recurrent episodes of pancreatitis or duodenal obstruction.
The surgeon takes down the hepatic flexure widely, using surgical instruments to facilitate later coccurization, with the surrounding anatomical structures visible in the surgical field.
After fully taking down the hepatic flexure, the surgeon obtains clear views of the duodenum, revealing its anatomical position and surrounding structures.
The annular pancreas is visible on the screen, causing a circumferential obstruction of the duodenum, with the pancreas encircling the duodenal structure.
The proximal part of the duodenum appears more dilated and is positioned without tension as mobilization progresses.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the duodenum, achieving near-complete corporalization, which allows for a clear view of the annular pancreas causing circumferential obstruction of the duodenum.
The proximal part of the duodenum, located near the annular pancreas, is observed to sit without tension.
The surgeon approximates the duodenal segment to the previously created Roux limb, positioning it without tension through a defect in the mesocolon, now located in the supracolic compartment.
The surgeon creates a two-layer anastomosis using robotic assistance, with the robotic arms visible docking to facilitate precise suturing.
The surgeon uses three ovicle sutures to create a posterior seromuscular row, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon aligns the limbs to form an isoperistaltic joint, ensuring proper orientation for optimal function.
The jejunum is positioned in the supracolic compartment in the proper orientation after completing the posterior aspect of the anastomosis.
Enterotomies are created on both the duodenum and jejunum limbs, with a visible posterior cuff of tissue prepared for stitching the back wall of the anastomosis.

The robotic hock cautery is used to create an enterotomy on both the dilated duodenum and jejunum, ensuring the openings are even while accounting for the tendency of these structures to expand under tension.
The surgeon uses Forovacral stitches to create a continuous anastomosis between the duodenum and jejunum, following the previously established posterior seromuscular row.

The surgeon stitches the back wall of the anastomosis first, ensuring that the mucosa is included with each suture. Subsequently, the anterior wall is stitched, with each suture grabbing the mucosa and inverting it.
The robotic system is utilized to perform the surgical technique, with visible manipulation of the jejunum in the supracolic compartment following the completion of the posterior stitching.
The surgeon uses a canal technique to further invert the mucosa during the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and uses four additional ovicral stitches to create a seromuscular layer similar to the posterior layer, reinforcing the anastomosis site.
The surgeon completes the formation of a two-layer side-to-side functional end-to-end duodenal jejunostomy in the supracolic compartment, with visible layers of tissue joined together.
